content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 6,
1993
Alan R.
Pendleton
Executive Director
San Francisco Bay Conservation
and Development Commission
Thirty Van Ness Avenue,
Suite 2011
San Francisco,
CA
94102-6080
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—93-283
Dear Mr. Pendleton:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding the conflict-of—interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act
(the “Act”) •1
Since your request seeks general
guidance,
we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance. 2
Please note that we do not provide advice with respect to
third parties.
Furthermore, nothing in this letter should be
construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken
place.
Also, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing
advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
Finally, please note that the Commission is currently
considering extensive amendments to the regulation which defines
“consultant,” Regulation 18700.
Accordingly, the law upon which
this advice is based may change in the near future.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—93-283
Page
2
QUESTIONS
1.
Is a scientific advisor who prepares an environmental
impact report for the San Francisco Bay Conservation and
Development Commission (the
“BCDC”) relating to an application for
a permit renewal submitted by Cargill Incorporated
(“Cargill”)
a
“consultant” under the Act?
2.
If the scientific advisor is considered a consultant,
and
therefore,
a public official,
may he or she participate in any
decision that will have a foreseeable material financial effect on
Cargill?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
It appears that the advisor will not function
independently of the BCDC’s control and direction.
Furthermore,
the contract you have provided for our review suggests that there
will be a contractual relationship between the advisor and the
BCDC.
Therefore, the advisor will be a consultant under the Act.
However, if there is significant intervening substantive
review performed,
as provided in the contract, the scientific
advisor will not be participating in governmental decisions.
Therefore, the advisor would not have disqualification
responsibilities with respect to the duties which relate to the
environmental impact report.
2.
As a “consultant” to the BCDC, the scientific advisor may
not participate in any decision that will have a foreseeable
material financial effect on any of his or her economic interests,
including Cargill.
FACTS
Cargill has submitted an application to the San Francisco
BCDC for a permit renewal relating to maintenance work associated
with the solar evaporation of salt.
To consider and evaluate all impacts on the environment
associated with the application for the permit, the BCDC is
required to prepare an environmental assessment as mandated under
the California Environmental Quality Act
(CEQA).
The BCDC must
circulate the environmental impact report
(EIR)
and allow for
public comment before it may act on Cargill’s application.
The BCDC and Cargill Salt,
a Division of Cargill
Incorporated, propose to enter into an agreement for the hiring of
a scientific advisor
3 to prepare the EIR.
The contract submitted
for our review provides that the advisor will prepare draft
The term “advisor” in this letter references the term
“consultant” in the contract you have provided for our review.


Our File No.
1-93-283
Page
3
documents, provide expertise,
and provide analysis of the extent
of potential impacts on the environment and recommendations on
ways to eliminate or reduce identified adverse impacts.
The contract further states that Cargill shall be responsible
for paying for all services performed by the advisor and other
associated charges related to the permit application.
The
scientific advisor will be hired and compensated by Cargill.
The
BCDC will not be responsible in any way for making any payments to
the advisor.
However,
no advisor shall be selected until the Executive
Director of the
BCDC agrees in writing that the proposed advisor
is acceptable to the BCDC.
The prospective advisor will be a
signatory to the contract which provides that the advisor agrees
to perform work in the manner described in the agreement and
agrees to accept the direction of the BCDC’s staff.
The contract generally provides that the advisor will closely
consult and coordinate with the BCDC.
For example, the advisor
will be under the direction of the Executive Director of the BCDC.
The contract further states that prior to public release or
review,
any document shall first be submitted to the Executive
Director with a request that it be reviewed and approved as
meeting the requirements of the applicable laws and regulations,
and as containing fair,
complete, objective,
and reliable
information.
However, the BCDC will retain ultimate authority and
responsibility for the completeness, accuracy, objectivity,
and
legal sufficiency of the environmental impact report.
In
fulfilling this obligation, the Executive Director on behalf of
the BCDC and the BCDC shall have the complete and unconditional
discretion to modify in any way, to substitute, or to edit any and
all of the material provided for public review.
In our conversation of July 30,
1993,
you stated that the
BCDC has staff experienced by way of education, training,
or
experience, other than the Executive Director,
who may review the
advisor’s work.
One person under consideration is Dr. Michael Josselyn of
Wetlands Research Associates,
Inc.
who is presently under contract
with the BCDC as a biological advisor.
Dr. Josselyn has
received more than $250 in income from Cargill within the last 12
months.
On the basis of your letter of September 3,
1993,
you
have determined that Dr. Josselyn is not presently a public
official under the Act.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using the official’s
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.
In addition, the Act requires


Our File No.
1-93-283
Page
4
every public official to disclose all the official’s economic
interests that could foreseeably be affected by the exercise of
the official’s duties.
(Sections 81002(c),
87200—87313.)
A “public official” is defined in Regulation 18700,
in
pertinent part,
as follows:
(a)
“Public official at any level of state or
local government” means every natural person who is
a member, officer,
employee,
or consultant of a
state or local government agency.
***
(2)
“Consultant” shall include any
natural person who provides under contract,
information, advice,
recommendation or counsel
to a state or local government agency,
provided, however, that “consultant” shall not
include a person who:
(A)
Conducts research and arrives
at conclusions with respect to his or her
rendition of information,
advice,
recommendation,
or counsel independent of
control and direction of the agency or
any agency official, other than normal
contract monitoring;
and
(B)
Possesses no authority with
respect to any agency decision beyond the
rendition of information,
advice, recom
mendation or counsel.
***
(c)
A public official or designated employee
“participates in the making of a governmental
decision” when, acting within the authority of his
or her position,
he or she:
(1)
Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity
or private person regarding the decision;
or
(2)
Advises or makes recommendations to
the decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review,
by:
(A)
Conducting research or making
any investigation which requires the
exercise of judgment on the part of the
official or designated employee and the


Our File No.
1-93-283
Page
5
purpose of which is to influence the
decision; or
(B)
Preparing or presenting any
report, analysis or opinion, orally or in
writing, which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of
which is to influence the decision.
This definition has been broadly interpreted to prevent
evasion of the conflict-of-interest safeguards of the Act by the
delegation of decision-making authority to private parties such as
consultants or independent contractors.
(See e.g.,
In re Maloney
(1977)
3
FPPC Ops.
69.)
The first question presented under our analysis is whether
the advisor provides information, advice,
recommendation,
or
counsel to BCDC.
(Regulation l8700(a)(2).)
The Commission has
previously advised that a person who prepares an EIR is presumed
to provide information,
advice,
recommendation,
or counsel to an
agency with respect to the EIR.
(ll Advice Letter,
No.
A—89—485.)
The second question is whether the two—part exception
provided in the regulation applies.
(Regulation 18700(a)(2),
(A)
and
(B).
This is a question of fact that will depend upon the
surrounding circumstances.
The Commission has advised that the first part of the two-
part test has been met if he or she is retained to use his or her
own judgment and expertise and his or her decisions are not
sublect to day-to-day review or direction by the agency.
In those
situations, the individual contracts to deliver a finished
product, just as any vendor of goods and services,
and not to
participate in or advise the agency on general governmental
decisions requiring expertise.
(Egan Advice Letter,
No.
1—91—111.)
Conversely, the Commission has found that this exclusion does
not apply when contractors to a government agency,
in essence, act
like “employees” and provide general advice on an ongoing basis
with duties like those normally performed by agency staff.
(In re
Maloney,
supra,
Egan Advice Letter, supra.)
In reaching a determination, the Commission has,
in essence,
concluded that it is necessary to examine the scope of activities
performed by the contractor.
The question to ask, then, is
whether the duties of the person retained by the public agency are
such as to bring them within the meaning of the term “consultant.”
(Workman Advice Letter,
No.
1-87-078.)
Here, the contract provides the advisor will prepare draft
documents, provide expertise and analysis,
and make various


Our File No.
1—93-283
Page
6
recommendations.
Specifically, the advisor will prepare the
following:
1)
a notice of preparation of an EIR;
2)
a draft EIR;
3) all documents associated with consultations with agencies
responsible for the federal and state Endangered Species Acts;
4)
summarize and respond to comments submitted by members of the
public;
and 5)
prepare supporting documentation as is deemed
necessary to prepare for a public hearing on the EIR.
However, the contract expressly provides for a significant
amount of control and direction by the Executive Director of the
BCDC.
For example, the advisor must agree to accept the direction
of the BCDC’s staff.
The advisor will be required to closely
consult and coordinate with the BCDC staff, specifically, the
Executive Director.
Thus, it appears that the advisor will be
subiect to day—to-day review or direction by the agency,
and the
first part of the exception (Regulation 18700 (a) (2) (A))
does not
apply.
Since both parts of the exception must be met, the
exception does not apply.
A third question is whether the consultant is being retained
under contract by the BCDC to prepare the EIR.
If the contract is
between the BCDC and Cargill only,
then the person is not a
consultant under the Act.
We have advised,
for example, that
where an engineer has no contract with the government agency to
perform work,
he or she is not a consultant under Regulation
18700(a)(2).
(Olsen Advice Letter,
No.
1—90—451.)
According to the contract you have submitted for our review,
the advisor who will be performing the work must agree to perform
specific services,
as noted above, for the BCDC pursuant to the
contract in question,
and the advisor will be a signatory to the
contract.
Thus, it appears from all the terms in the contract
that there is a contractual relationship between the advisor and
the
BCDC for purposes of the Act.
A final question is whether there will be “significant
intervening substantive review.”
(Section 82019(c), Regulation
18700(c).)
If so, the scientific advisor would not be
participating in the making of a government decision,
and he or
she would have no disqualification obligations with respect to the
EIR.
Significant intervening substantive review has been narrowly
interpreted.
For example, it has been interpreted to require more
than the mere review of the recommendations by superiors,
but
rather the independent checking of the results without solely
relying on the data of the consultant.
(Kaplan Advice Letter,
No. A-82-108,
Greenwald Advice Letter,
No.
1-90—349.)
Furthermore,
a consultant participates in a decision,
even if it
is “reviewed” by several of his or her superiors, if those
superiors rely on the data or analysis prepared by the consultant
without checking it independently, if they rely on the
professional judgment of the advisor,
or if the consultant in some
other way actually may influence the final decision.


Our File No.
1—93—283
Page 7
Based on the terms of your contract, prior to public release
or review,
any document prepared by the consultant shall first be
submitted to the Executive Director with a request that it be
reviewed and approved as meeting the requirements of the
applicable laws and regulations,
and that it contain fair,
complete, objective,
and reliable information.
According to your
facts, the BCDC has staff experienced by way of education,
training,
or experience, other than the Executive Director,
who
may review the advisor’s work.
Furthermore, the Executive Director on behalf of the BCDC and
the
BCDC shall have the complete and unconditional discretion to
modify in any way, to substitute,
or to edit any and all of the
material provided for public review.
The BCDC will retain the
ultimate authority and responsibility for the completeness,
accuracy, objectivity,
and legal sufficiency of the EIR.
Thus, if the extent of review is consistent with the provisions of
the contract, it appears there will be significant intervening
substantive review of the advisor’s work.
Conflict—of—Interest Provisions
Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
4 that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a
member of his or her immediate family or on any source of income,
aggregating $250 or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made.
(Section 87103(c).)
A public official under the Act would be prohibited from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
5
An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not
required; however, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is
not reasonably foreseeable.
(Downey Cares v.
Downey Community
Development Comm.
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983,
989-991; Witt v.
Morrow
(1977)
70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
You state in your letter dated July 20,
1993, that the Act
appears to prohibit the BCDC from using Dr. Josselyn in light of
the payment Dr.
Josselyn has recently received from Cargill.
Please note that the Act does not prohibit an agency from hiring
any person.
Rather, it seeks to inhibit conduct where a public
official’s economic interests would be affected.


Our File No.
1—93—283
Page
8
You stated that one consultant under consideration is
Dr. Michael Josselyn of Wetlands Research Associates, Inc.,
who is
presently under contract with the BCDC as a biological advisor.
Dr. Josselyn has received more than $250 in income from Cargill
within the last
12 months.
You indicate in your letter of
September 3,
1993, that based on previous discussions with
Commission staff,
Dr. Josselyn is not treated as a public official
pursuant to Regulation 18700 (a) (2) •6
If Dr. Josselyn were selected to perform the work,
he would
be prohibited from participating in decisions which have a
reasonably foreseeable and material effect on Cargill because
Cargill has been a source of income to him of more than $250
within the last
12 months.
If these are the facts,
he may not
prepare the EIR for the Cargill application because it is
foreseeable that the report will have a material financial effect
on Cargill,
a source of income to Dr. Josselyn.
7
However, if
there is “significant intervening substantive review,” as
noted above,
he may prepare the EIR report since he would not be
participating in the making of governmental decisions.
If you have any questions please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322_5901.B
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Me chaca
Counsel,
Legal Division
6
Oral advice does not provide immunity from enforcement.
(Section 83114,
Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
You have asked us to review the contract as it relates to
Section 87103.6.
Section 87103.6 does not appear relevant to your
inquiry.
If Cargill and the BCDC were to select Dr. Josselyn,
Cargill would be considered a source of income to Dr. Josselyn by
virtue of a previous employment relationship.
Therefore,
application of Section 87103.6 would not reduce the potential for
a conflict of interest.
8
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10c per page.

QUESTION: 1.
Is a scientific advisor who prepares an environmental
impact report for the San Francisco Bay Conservation and
Development Commission (the
“BCDC”) relating to an application for
a permit renewal submitted by Cargill Incorporated
(“Cargill”)
a
“consultant” under the Act?
2.
If the scientific advisor is considered a consultant,
and
therefore,
a public official,
may he or she participate in any
decision that will have a foreseeable material financial effect on
Cargill?

CONCLUSION: 1.
It appears that the advisor will not function
independently of the BCDC’s control and direction.
Furthermore,
the contract you have provided for our review suggests that there
will be a contractual relationship between the advisor and the
BCDC.
Therefore, the advisor will be a consultant under the Act.
However, if there is significant intervening substantive
review performed,
as provided in the contract, the scientific
advisor will not be participating in governmental decisions.
Therefore, the advisor would not have disqualification
responsibilities with respect to the duties which relate to the
environmental impact report.
2.

not participate in any decision that will have a foreseeable
material financial effect on any of his or her economic interests,
including Cargill.

Furthermore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. Also, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Finally, please note that the Commission is currently considering extensive amendments to the regulation which defines “consultant,” Regulation 18700. Accordingly, the law upon which this advice is based may change in the near future. 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18329(c) (3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. 1—93-283 Page 2 QUESTIONS 1. Is a scientific advisor who prepares an environmental impact report for the San Francisco Bay Conservation and Development Commission (the “BCDC”) relating to an application for a permit renewal submitted by Cargill Incorporated (“Cargill”) a “consultant” under the Act? 2. If the scientific advisor is considered a consultant, and therefore, a public official, may he or she participate in any decision that will have a foreseeable material financial effect on Cargill? CONCLUSIONS 1. It appears that the advisor will not function independently of the BCDC’s control and direction. Furthermore, the contract you have provided for our review suggests that there will be a contractual relationship between the advisor and the BCDC. Therefore, the advisor will be a consultant under the Act. However, if there is significant intervening substantive review performed, as provided in the contract, the scientific advisor will not be participating in governmental decisions. Therefore, the advisor would not have disqualification responsibilities with respect to the duties which relate to the environmental impact report. 2. As a “consultant” to the BCDC, the scientific advisor may not participate in any decision that will have a foreseeable material financial effect on any of his or her economic interests, including Cargill.
